responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit operates at the intersection of three competing demands: maximizing naturalistic behavior capture (requiring domestic atmosphere) while maintaining safety and clinical rigor (requiring infrastructure and staffing), sustaining longitudinal enrollment (requiring participant acceptance and funding continuity) while managing false positives and annotation burden (requiring algorithmic triage), and maintaining academic independence (requiring full publication control) while securing pharma partnerships (requiring negotiated access). The levers below target these core tensions—environment versus clinical safety, event scarcity versus operational cost, data richness versus privacy, automation versus human validation, and funding stability versus stakeholder alignment—because resolving them determines whether the facility can sustainably capture rare parasomnia events and produce credible phenotyping science.

levers.name: Residential atmosphere versus clinical safety infrastructure trade-off
levers.consequences: The facility must balance a domestic feel that elicits naturalistic parasomnia behavior against the safety systems (padded walls, door alarms, restricted windows) that suppress it or create artificiality. Installing and maintaining safety hardware may inadvertently cue participants to abnormal expectation, altering sleep architecture and parasomnia expression; conversely, minimizing visible safety measures increases injury risk if an event escalates. This tension directly affects data validity—if the environment feels too institutional, capture rates drop and Aim 2 phenotyping becomes underpowered.
options: Employ concealed safety engineering (soft-edge handrails disguised as décor elements, motion-responsive floor lighting positioned as ambient features, door locking on the exterior only) that protects without telegraphing surveillance or constraint, accepted after ethics review and explicit participant consent on setup.

Install visible but normalized safety fixtures (foam corner guards in styles and colors matching interior design, transparent acrylic window films, openly disclosed monitoring) as part of the intake briefing so participants mentally integrate them as standard sleep-lab convention rather than aberration.

Stratify suites into two operational tiers—four suites with maximal concealment for primary phenotyping cohort, and four with standard clinical safety—then compare event yield and sleep-stage metrics between tiers to empirically measure the atmospheric penalty and guide year-two design decisions.
levers.review_lever: Minimizing visible safety measures trades short-term realism against long-term liability: if a participant suffers a preventable injury because safety systems were too subtle, ethics approval and institutional trust collapse. None of the options address the deeper problem—staff perception of 'acceptable risk' in a residential facility is more conservative than in a traditional hospital sleep lab, so even well-designed concealment may not overcome technician reluctance to reduce supervision during high-risk events.

levers.name: Event scarcity and capture efficiency versus cost-per-productive-night
levers.consequences: The inclusion criterion (≥2 episodes per month) implies an event density of roughly 0.07 episodes per night population-wide, yet many participants may have zero captured episodes during their stay, necessitating extension or replacement. Each unproductive admission consumes fixed overhead (suite utilities, staffing, technician annotation time) without yielding scientifically usable data; the protocol permits up to 20% unproductive stays before mandatory criteria review. This lever directly determines bed utilization, cost per captured event, and whether year-two scaling to 30–35 participants is economically sustainable.
options: Implement a two-phase admission strategy: first 7–10 nights under routine monitoring to measure actual event frequency in the residential setting, then gate stay extension on a documented episode or confirmed high-risk movement signature from mattress sensors, capping extensions only for participants showing clear signal.

Establish a pre-admission remote actigraphy and sleep diary screening period (4–6 weeks prior) to quantify home baseline episode frequency and refine inclusion thresholds, rejecting candidates whose actual rate falls below 1 episode per month and reserving extension beds only for participants whose home data predicted higher yield.

Adopt a rolling-cohort model with a standing 'event-capture reserve pool' of 5–10 pre-screened participants on 2–3 week call-up intervals, deployed only when active suites flag high-yield signals (e.g., mattress-sensor detected movement clusters matching parasomnia signature), maximizing event density per admission.
levers.review_lever: Tightening pre-admission screening to reduce unproductive stays risks excluding participants with genuinely variable parasomnia (intermittent responders whose month-to-month frequency is unpredictable), potentially biasing the Aim 2 phenotyping cohort toward high-frequency easy cases. The call-up reserve pool assumes reliable short-notice participant availability, which recruitment in a regional population may not support; missed call-ups generate no revenue and strain participant goodwill.

levers.name: Data richness and physiological resolution versus privacy constraint and public deposit feasibility
levers.consequences: The tiered sensing model (nightly low-burden plus scheduled enhanced PSG plus escalation nights) maximizes event resolution but generates multimodal data including bedroom video, which carries legal and ethical restrictions that bar public repository deposit. Retaining bedroom video only for on-site annotation limits reproducibility and external validation of event-triage algorithms, since future researchers cannot audit the video-labeled ground truth; removing video entirely preserves privacy but sacrifices the gold-standard annotation resource for rare, morphologically complex events. This tension directly undermines Aim 3 benchmarking credibility.
options: Restrict video to the first night only (establishing baseline movement patterns and technician familiarity) and rely entirely on dual-rater PSG annotation plus mattress-sensor correlation for all subsequent events, depositing de-identified EEG, actigraphy, and computed event-flags in a public repository and offering conditional video access under a formal data use agreement reviewed case-by-case.

Implement a federated two-tier sharing model: deposit all low-burden sensor streams (EEG headband, mattress pressure, infrared body-position maps without face resolution, ambient sound spectrograms) openly; reserve full PSG and video in a secure institutional repository accessible to external collaborators upon ethics approval and institutional affiliation verification, documented in the public dataset as 'extended protocol available'.

Omit bedroom video entirely and substitute a second independent technician live-coding parasomnia morphology in real time (body position, limb movement, vocalization intensity, coherence with EEG arousal) at the bedside for the final-night and escalation sessions, storing only the coded behavioral descriptors rather than raw video, shareable under standard data use agreements.
levers.review_lever: Removing video sacrifices nuance for events with ambiguous polysomnographic signatures (shallow arousals with prominent motor behavior that may be confusional arousal versus sleep-related movement disorder boundary case)—human scorers often resolve these by replaying video frame-by-frame. The federated model extends legal review timelines for external access and may deter replication studies; it also assumes the institutional repository remains actively stewarded for 10+ years, a commitment often abandoned post-funding.

levers.name: Autonomous event detection capability versus maintaining human scorer primacy
levers.consequences: Aim 3 explicitly benchmarks semi-automated event-triage tools against dual human scoring, not against 'ground truth' diagnosis, because parasomnia phenotyping is not yet standardized enough to claim algorithmic autonomy. However, if triage tools achieve high sensitivity and specificity (e.g., 85% agreement with human scorers), pressure will mount from funders, clinicians, and future industry partners to position them as diagnostically autonomous. Caving to this pressure risks publishing inflated performance claims on data that reflects human annotation variance rather than true accuracy, damaging credibility when the tool is prospectively tested on new populations.
options: Explicitly frame the triage model as a 'technician-assistance tool' in all publications and grant proposals, reporting only sensitivity and specificity relative to majority human-rater agreement and refusing to claim autonomous detection; design validation workflow to require one human reviewer to approve triage flags before clinical reporting, regardless of algorithm confidence.

Prospectively reserve a held-out test set (20% of captured events, withheld from model training) and at month 30 conduct a parallel annotation by a new independent human rater blind to both the algorithm output and the original dual-rater consensus, using four-way agreement patterns (algorithm, original pair, held-out rater) to transparently quantify where the model diverges from human consensus.

Publish two parallel results: one emphasizing triage sensitivity for identifying potential events (enabling technician review prioritization) and a separate report on specificity for ruling out false positives, clearly labeling each metric's clinical utility and limitations rather than combining them into a single 'accuracy' statistic that obscures conditional performance.
levers.review_lever: Insisting on human primacy may under-utilize the computational model and frustrate collaborators expecting digital automation; conversely, overselling the model's maturity now creates pressure to deploy it clinically before adequate prospective validation, and sets up replication failure in external populations where the human-annotation style or event prevalence differs. The held-out rater strategy requires securing a third expert scorer for month 30, contingent on continued funding and their availability.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical partnership structure and academic independence
levers.consequences: A pharma partner can accelerate funding and provide domain expertise, but the default negotiated arrangement (30-day pre-publication review window only) is silent on downstream IP—what if a pharma-sponsored secondary analysis identifies a novel biomarker or therapeutic target? Ambiguity here may lead to post-hoc disputes over publication rights, institutional review, or derivative patent claims. The current framing correctly positions pharma as 'optional upside,' but failing to lock in governance terms upfront risks the partnership becoming a lever for funders or the sponsor to influence publication strategy under the guise of 'collaborative science.'
options: Establish a written Partnership Charter at engagement, specifying that all primary Aims 1–3 data, publications, and IP remain wholly the University's unless pharma co-authors manuscripts (triggering standard co-authorship IP sharing); pharma receives 30-day review, but University retains unilateral publication decision and all rights to methodological patents on the triage tool.

Decline any pharma partnership during the 3-year pilot and secure full funding via DFG + University internal mechanisms, then solicit industry co-investment only for a separate Phase Two protocol (year 4 onwards) targeting specific biomarkers or patient stratification, where pharma involvement is scoped upfront as a distinct study.

Offer pharma a tiered engagement: Year 1 sponsorship of the event-triage algorithm development (Aim 3) as a discrete task with co-authorship opportunity but no veto on primary-aim publications; Year 2 optional participation in a sub-study using residual samples for novel biomarker discovery, with pharma bearing full cost and University retaining IP on any diagnostically-actionable findings.
levers.review_lever: Rejecting pharma entirely in a 3-year, €3.8M budget assumes DFG success in the 2026 cycle (currently uncertain) and forgoes potential acceleration; accepting pharma without a Charter risks subtle mission drift, where Year 2 planning unconsciously prioritizes phenotypes valuable to the sponsor over those most scientifically novel. The tiered model divides effort across two protocols, creating annotation and ethics-review parallelism overhead.

levers.name: Annotation throughput and inter-rater agreement sustainability
levers.consequences: The protocol mandates dual independent scoring of all PSG nights using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported; for 50–70 participants with an average of 3 enhanced PSG nights each (first, mid-stay, final), plus escalation nights for captured events, the scoring burden could exceed 400–500 nights of full PSG annotation. At €40–60 per night for expert scorers (clinical neurophysiologists), annual scoring costs alone are €16K–30K year one, rising to €25K–50K year two. If inter-rater agreement falls below 85% (Cohen's kappa), the protocol requires adjudication by a third party, further inflating cost. This lever directly determines whether Aim 2 phenotyping analysis is feasible and timely.
options: Implement a stepped annotation protocol: year-one nights scored by the two core postdocs (building internal agreement through biweekly consensus meetings), and only nights with disagreement >5% (kappa <0.80) are sent to external expert scorers for adjudication, then conduct pooled inter-rater reliability at year-end to justify or adjust the threshold.

Develop a hybrid annotation workflow where the night technician performs real-time AASM-aligned flagging during the recording (marking probable arousals, movement onsets, microarousals), then two independent offline scorers review the technician-flagged windows only (not the entire night), with technician confidence attached to each flag to weight scorer priority; kappa is computed on the flagged-window subset.

Outsource all scoring to a contracted sleep-medicine clinic partner (e.g., a regional hospital with existing PSG-scoring infrastructure) under a per-night fee-for-service agreement and a shared protocol checklist, trading some quality-control overhead for predictable monthly costs and separation of operational and scientific roles, with monthly spot-check agreement audits.
levers.review_lever: Relying on postdocs for primary scoring works in year one but jeopardizes continuity if either postdoc leaves; the biweekly consensus process slows annotation velocity and may inadvertently create a third 'reference' scorer that inflates apparent agreement. The hybrid technician-flagging model reduces offline scoring time but risks embedding technician bias into the flagging layer, systematically improving agreement in unhelpful ways (technician and scorers converging on the same mistake). Outsourcing saves salary costs but surrenders day-to-day quality oversight and assumes the partner clinic has consistent AASM expertise—regional variation in sleep-stage scoring is real.

levers.name: Phased suite expansion and demand forecasting versus upfront capital commitment
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites pending year-one data on event-capture rates, staffing safety, and annotation throughput; however, if recruitment demand exceeds 8-suite capacity and a waiting list forms, the facility sacrifices scientifically important participants and funding opportunity in year two. Conversely, expanding to 12 suites prematurely locks in additional lease and staffing costs (€80K–120K annually) if participant flow remains modest. This lever determines facility utilization efficiency and year-two budget realism.
options: Commit to full 12-suite fit-out in year-one renovation (requiring €150K–200K additional capital for cabling, monitor infrastructure, safety systems) but operationalize only 8 suites initially; pre-wire the four dark suites and conduct monthly utilization forecasting (queued referrals, event-capture yield, staff utilization) with a formal decision gate at month 12 to activate suites 9–12 if capacity utilization exceeds 75% and event-capture rate meets Aim 2 thresholds.

Build out only the 8-suite core in year-one and execute a streamlined decision protocol at month 18: if enrolled participants >30, queued referrals >10, and captured events >50, trigger a 6-month expansion contract to add suites 9–12 by month 24, accepting a 6-month waiting period for incremental participants rather than forecasting demand upfront.

Negotiate with the residential property landlord a contingent lease amendment allowing month-by-month suite occupancy scaling (e.g., rent billed per active suite used rather than per total available), eliminating capital commitment to unused infrastructure and allowing suite additions on 60-day notice without construction, traded against higher per-suite lease cost (5–10% premium).
levers.review_lever: Pre-wiring all 12 suites avoids construction disruption mid-study but commits €150K+ even if demand materializes slowly, potentially exhausting year-one contingency funds. The rolling decision-gate approach respects uncertainty but may find that activation at month 18 comes too late to meaningfully expand year-two cohort size (ethics amendments, participant recruitment pipeline, staff hiring all require 2–3 months lead time). The per-suite variable lease preserves flexibility but higher marginal rent may erode margins and give landlord perverse incentive to deny expansion notice.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates five core tensions: safety infrastructure versus naturalistic home-like conditions (clinical monitoring will suppress the spontaneous behaviors being studied), event rarity versus long residential stays (extended observation captures rare events but strains participant burden and budget), physiological resolution versus privacy protection (video footage provides rich behavioral context but creates insurmountable public-sharing barriers), staffing coverage versus operational cost (24/7 night shifts for 8 suites demand expensive rotating teams), and methodological rigor versus timeline pressure (dual-rater scoring and algorithm benchmarking consume 18+ months while funding gates trigger at month 18). The seven levers below target these core conflicts by forcing explicit choices about which trade-offs to accept and which to mitigate through operational design.

levers.name: Capturing rare events without extended institutional stays
levers.consequences: Longer residential stays (2–8 weeks) maximize event yield but create substantial participant burden, restrict recruitment to highly motivated individuals, and occupy bed capacity longer per enrolled subject. Conversely, shorter standardized stays reduce operational friction but risk unproductive admissions — if the target 70–80% capture rate drops below 50%, the facility consumes resources without scientific return, and the month-18 failure trigger (fewer than 40 adjudicated events) becomes harder to meet.
options: Mandate a tiered stay protocol: all participants begin with a baseline 2-week stay; only those with zero captured events extend to week 3–8, with extension approval conditional on technician-flagged candidate events occurring by the end of week 2 (preserving bed turnover while retargeting high-variance individuals).

Recruit a parallel remote-monitoring cohort using home-deployed dry EEG and mattress sensors without facility residence, accepting degraded physiological resolution in exchange for larger denominator and longer natural-history observation windows to feed the event-triage algorithm even if formal event adjudication remains limited to in-facility stays.

Implement weekly in-facility 'capture sessions' lasting 3–4 consecutive nights with participants returning to daily life between sessions over 6–8 weeks total, attempting to harvest the behavioral ecology of home while maintaining facility control and compressing total bed occupancy per participant.
levers.review_lever: Rare-event capture is fundamentally probabilistic and participant-dependent; the plan assumes 70–80% yield based on a self-reported 2-episodes-per-month screening criterion, but this criterion may overestimate true frequency in a new unfamiliar environment (novel setting often suppresses parasomnia expression). None of the options directly addresses whether the pre-admission assessment pipeline can accurately predict which screened individuals will actually produce events, risking systematic over-recruitment of low-yield participants and burndown of the €80-per-night compensation budget.

levers.name: Technician safety workload and event-response capability
levers.consequences: Covering 8 active suites with three rotating night technicians (one per shift, two off) provides adequate margin for annotation and event intervention, but scales poorly if suites expand or event frequency is higher than projected. Understaffing creates annotation backlog and delayed event escalation; overstaffing during low-event periods wastes personnel budget. The plan does not specify a target response time from technician alert to bedside, which affects clinical safety and event data completeness.
options: Lock the technician-to-suite ratio at 1:4 (one technician per 4 active suites) and conduct quarterly safety audits; if actual event rates exceed one event per technician per night on average, hire an additional part-time overnight shift supervisor rather than expanding suites, accepting that suite expansion is deferred indefinitely until staffing capacity is demonstrably surplus.

Deploy a hybrid model where a trained sleep technician covers 6–8 suites for clinical safety and immediate escalation, with a separate part-time data annotator (0.5 FTE, daytime) handling real-time scoring review and flagging ambiguous cases for dual-scorer triage, shifting annotation burden away from the critical-path overnight shift.

Establish a pre-agreed call-list of on-site emergency physician or neurologist coverage for nights when event frequency exceeds one per technician, formalized in an MOU with the affiliated hospital's neurology department, ensuring clinical escalation capacity without permanent headcount addition.
levers.review_lever: The plan specifies three research technicians but does not define event-response protocols or acceptable response latency; 'safe staffing ratios' is cited as a month-18 success criterion but no numerical target is stated. None of the options explicitly prices in the cognitive load of real-time AASM-criteria scoring during a live event (distinguishing sleep terror from confusional arousal while the participant is confused is cognitively demanding), and all three assume the technician can adequately monitor video feeds and sensor streams simultaneously, which may degrade under fatigue during weeks of low-event nights.

levers.name: Dual-rater independence and annotation throughput sustainability
levers.consequences: Requiring two independent raters to score all captured events ensures inter-rater reliability and AASM-criteria rigor, but at a high labor cost per hour: typical dual-rater video-EEG scoring requires 4–8 hours per hour of recorded content depending on signal complexity. With 8 suites running continuously, even at a 40% capture rate, dual scoring of all nights (versus selective nights) can quickly exhaust budget and delay algorithmic development (Aim 3) which depends on complete labeled datasets.
options: Score only first-night, mid-stay, and final-night PSG montages in full dual-rater mode, treating those as the 'gold standard' events; technician-flagged candidate events on low-burden nights receive single-rater screening with dual-rater adjudication triggered only for algorithm-evaluation cohorts, accepting that longitudinal event frequency estimates will rely on technician flagging rather than formal dual scoring.

Contract an external sleep medicine center to provide the second independent rater, shifting annotation cost into a fixed subcontract and decoupling the facility's operational team from the scoring bottleneck; publish inter-rater agreement quarterly and declare analysis frozen if agreement falls below a pre-specified threshold (e.g., 80% concordance on event presence/absence).

Implement a three-tier annotation hierarchy: tier-one rapid technician triage (event present/absent/ambiguous), tier-two single-rater formal scoring for all flagged events, tier-three dual-rater adjudication only for a random 30% of scored events plus all disagreements, using agreement from the sampled subset to extrapolate inter-rater reliability across the full dataset.
levers.review_lever: Dual-rater scoring is scientifically necessary for validity but the plan does not specify how many events are expected to be captured overall or whether the budget (€2M personnel over 3 years) allocates sufficient independent-rater time for full coverage. None of the options accounts for rater fatigue or drift over months of repetitive scoring, both of which will degrade agreement; the plan also assumes that the 'two independent raters' are readily available, but in a small sleep medicine field with limited expertise in parasomnia video analysis, finding truly independent, available raters may be operationally difficult within the Bonn region.

levers.name: Semi-automated event-triage algorithm training scope and cross-validation strategy
levers.consequences: Developing a benchmarked semi-automated triage tool (Aim 3) requires a labeled training set of captured-event cases; larger training sets improve algorithm generalization, but also demand extended data collection and dual-rater labeling time. Training only on facility events risks overfitting to facility-specific sensor noise and participant behaviors, whereas incorporating the remote-monitoring cohort (if adopted) provides diversity but introduces label ambiguity since remote events cannot be formally adjudicated in real time.
options: Restrict algorithm training to dual-rated in-facility events only, capping training set size at the number of events captured during the 3-year pilot; publish algorithm performance as facility-specific (sensitivity/specificity against dual-rater agreement on the captured cohort) and position the method as a 'prototype semi-automated event-flagging pipeline,' deferring multi-site external validation to a future phase-two grant.

Prospectively enroll 20–25 event-free control participants in the facility under identical protocols, generating a large labeled dataset of false-positive sensor activity and non-parasomnia arousals; train the algorithm on a balanced dataset of parasomnia events versus negative controls, enabling threshold optimization that explicitly minimizes false positives and improving real-world applicability.

Collaborate with a European sleep medicine consortium (e.g., Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin member centers) to share anonymized event clips and dual-rater scores, pooling training data across multiple sites in exchange for co-authorship and algorithm access; accept reduced publication timeline (algorithm development extends to month 30) but gain multi-site generalization.
levers.review_lever: The plan states the algorithm will be 'evaluated against dual human scorer agreement,' but does not specify whether the facility will reserve a held-out test set or if all captured events will be used for both training and evaluation, risking inflated performance estimates. Outcome class imbalance (many non-events, few parasomnia events) will skew standard metrics, and none of the options explicitly addresses how to handle ambiguous events (technician-flagged but not confirmed at dual review), which may represent the highest-value targets for algorithm assistance yet carry no ground truth.

levers.name: Privacy constraints and public data repository deposit feasibility
levers.consequences: Bedroom video is explicitly excluded from public repositories due to privacy risk, but this limits the richness of openly-shared data and reduces scientific transparency and reproducibility. Physiological-only deposits (EEG, sensors, arousal annotations) are feasible but provide no context for validating event classification, potentially limiting downstream reuse by other groups. The plan does not specify which physiological data will be deposited (full PSG, headband-only, or sensor subset) or timelines for de-identification and repository submission.
options: Deposit only de-identified low-burden nightly headband EEG, mattress sensor, and acoustic event annotations (without raw video) in a public BIDS repository, enabling algorithm reuse and longitudinal pattern analysis while preserving participant privacy; acknowledge in publications that behavioral context is unavailable and external researchers cannot independently validate event morphology from video.

Create a tiered-access data enclave: deposit anonymized physiological data and event summaries in a public repository, but house raw video and detailed event clips in a managed access portal (e.g., European Genome-Phenome Archive) requiring data-use agreements and ethics approval per request, reducing privacy risk while preserving scientific scrutiny.

Obtain explicit broad consent during enrollment for video deposit under a participant-controlled anonymization option: offer participants the choice to permit video sharing with face blurring and identifier removal, and deposit only from opt-in participants; expect 30–40% opt-in rate and deposit a smaller but fully transparent video-enriched dataset, framing partial transparency as superior to full opacity.
levers.review_lever: The plan identifies video privacy as a 'severe constraint' and bans public video deposit, but does not specify how bedroom context will be documented for future replication or how external researchers will validate event-classification disagreements; this trades transparency for privacy protection. None of the options explores whether the institutional review board or ethics committee would permit conditional video sharing under strong anonymization (face-blurred, temporally cropped), and the third option assumes 30–40% opt-in consent is achievable without evidence from similar parasomnia cohorts, which tend toward lower video-sharing acceptance due to the intimate nature of the setting.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical industry partnership timing and scope versus academic independence messaging
levers.consequences: An optional pharma partnership can provide upside funding and potential drug-testing collaboration, but introduces contractual obligations (e.g., the 30-day pre-publication review window) that may delay publication and create perception of industry bias even if academically protected. Entering into partnership early (year 1) secures funding but locks in requirements; delaying partnership until year 2 or 3 risks losing interested sponsors but preserves autonomy longer and allows methodological validation first.
options: Decline pharma partnership at launch and pursue DFG plus University of Bonn internal funding exclusively; preserve unrestricted publication timeline and frame the facility as purely academic-mission-driven in recruitment materials, maximizing participant trust; revisit pharma collaboration only after methods-validation publications (Aim 1) establish credibility, positioning the facility as a de-risk scientific partner rather than a captive contract-research site.

Solicit a pharma partnership letter of intent by month 6, specifying that funding comes in year 2 only and contingent on clearance by the scientific advisory board; draft a partnership agreement that guarantees publication rights after 60 days regardless of pharma comment, and require pharma to co-fund only non-core operational costs (e.g., algorithm development, control-group expansion) rather than core safety and enrollment infrastructure.

Establish a separate RBD-specific research track formally co-sponsored by a neurology-focused pharmaceutical partner (neurodegenerative disease interest), isolating industry involvement to the secondary RBD protocol and keeping the primary NREM parasomnia track entirely under DFG/university funding; this compartmentalization preserves academic independence claims for the main cohort while accepting industry involvement in an exploratory arm.
levers.review_lever: The plan positions pharma partnership as 'optional upside' and claims the academic team 'retains full publication rights,' but does not specify whether the 30-day comment window creates a de facto publication veto (if pharma objects, will the team proceed despite objection?), nor does it address how participants and recruitment networks will perceive industry involvement if announced during enrollment. The third option assumes that compartmentalizing the RBD arm will prevent spillover perception of bias, but in the field, reputation attaches to the entire research unit regardless of funding source segmentation.

levers.name: Facility expansion decision gates and participant demand forecasting
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites pending pilot success, but does not specify which success criteria unlock expansion (month 18 triggers review of enrollment <25 or event capture <40, but neither directly addresses suite utilization). If demand exceeds 8-suite capacity before month 18, the facility cannot scale and risks losing recruited participants; if demand is weak, expansion wastes capital on infrastructure that remains idle. Recruitment projections (15–20 participants year 1, 30–35 year 2) assume constant intake rate without explaining seasonal variation or recruitment ramp-up.
options: Adopt a rolling-enrollment expansion trigger: commit to 12-suite renovation by month 15 if enrollment reaches 20 participants by month 12 and technician safety audits confirm no safety deficits at 8-suite staffing; stagger the renovation across months 15–18 to minimize operational disruption and accept the risk that expansion capital may be committed before outcome-quality metrics (inter-rater agreement, algorithm performance) are final.

Implement a capacity-management protocol using a 6-month rolling wait-list: if prospective participants exceed 8-suite capacity, place them on a publicly-tracked waitlist with priority based on event frequency and clinical urgency; measure wait-list length monthly and trigger expansion discussion if >10 individuals are waiting; this defers expansion capital until demand is demonstrably sustained, but risks participant attrition if waits exceed 4 months.

Negotiate an option with the landlord or adjacent property owner to secure a third building (4 additional suites) with lease commencement at month 18, contingent on a go/no-go decision informed by month-18 external review data; this preserves expansion optionality without upfront capital commitment but reduces timing control and risks losing the property option if expansion criteria are not met by month 18.
levers.review_lever: Recruitment projections (15–20 year 1, 30–35 year 2) show a 50–75% ramp, but do not account for seasonal variation in parasomnia event reporting, delay between referral and enrollment (specialist assessment, screening PSG, pre-admission workup can take 2–3 months), or the fact that the NREM parasomnia population in Germany is poorly characterized, making demand forecasting speculative. None of the options addresses whether the pre-admission exclusion criteria (no nocturnal epilepsy, no untreated sleep apnea, no severe psychiatric instability) will narrow the referred pool more than expected, and the month-18 external review is the only circuit-breaker for course correction, which may be too late to salvage expansion timelines if capacity constraints emerge earlier.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia unit must navigate three foundational tensions: balancing domestic atmosphere with safety infrastructure (required by ethics and liability but threatens naturalistic behavior capture), managing infrequent event yields across extended stays without inflating costs or participant burden, and maintaining human oversight of event detection while reducing annotation workload through semi-automated tools. The selected levers address how to allocate capital and staffing across these competing demands, sequence participant recruitment against event-capture targets, structure the sensor escalation model to minimize false alarms, and position the facility's expansion trajectory within uncertain demand forecasting. These decisions collectively determine whether the facility achieves scientific credibility and operational sustainability within the 3-year funding window.

levers.name: Domestic ambiance preservation versus safety-driven renovation depth
levers.consequences: Extensive safety modifications (padded corridors, restricted windows, silent alarms, fire-rated materials) reduce risk and satisfy ethics committees but introduce clinical cues that may suppress naturalistic parasomnia expression. Conversely, minimal intervention preserves authentic sleep environment and behavior capture fidelity but increases liability exposure and may jeopardize recruitment if families perceive inadequate protection.
options: Implement staged renovation with core safety interventions (window restrictors, discrete corridor padding, inaudible alarm systems) phased in before participant arrival rather than completed during setup, allowing naturalistic baseline photography and family reassurance messaging to emphasize 'home-like with safety overlays' rather than 'clinical conversion'.

Employ removable or concealable safety infrastructure—magnetic corner guards, portable mattress rails, temporary threshold modifications—installed immediately before check-in so suites photograph and feel residential for marketing, then decorate with family-comfort elements (artwork, personal refrigerator, soft lighting) that dominate first visual impression.

Accept higher perceived clinical finish in the common areas (reception, monitoring station, dining) but invest disproportionately in suite interiors with residential furnishings, artwork, and acoustic treatments so participants spend private hours in non-institutional space, segregating the 'clinical' workflow from sleeping quarters.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that ethics committees demand observable safety while participant recruitment messaging relies on feeling 'not like a hospital.' The options all assume removable or perceptual tactics work, but none address the hard constraint that fire safety codes in a converted residential building may mandate irreversible structural changes (e.g., fire-rated door frames, wall treatments) that cannot be hidden; this could force a choice between passing inspection and preserving ambiance, not both.

levers.name: Escalation-night deployment frequency versus false-alarm cost containment
levers.consequences: Deploying full PSG immediately after every technician-flagged event captures clinical-grade detail but generates high sensor logistics overhead, technician fatigue from rapid equipment staging, and analyst cost for processing nocturnal false alarms (snoring, restless legs, normal arousals). Conversely, rationing escalation nights to only high-confidence flags reduces cost but risks missing secondary or subtle recurrences that refine phenotyping.
options: Implement a mandatory 24-hour review window before escalation-night decision: technician flags events nocturnally, then morning raw-data review by the clinical psychologist and PI determines escalation; this adds one morning interpretation step but filters low-confidence signals and concentrates PSG nights on episodes with higher post-hoc confirmation probability.

Establish participant-specific escalation thresholds during week one based on each person's typical false-alarm rate and event morphology, then automatically schedule full PSG after every N-th flagged event for that participant; this personalizes cost allocation and captures recurrence patterns without human gate-keeping each decision.

Restrict full PSG escalation nights to events captured on already-scheduled enhanced nights (first night, mid-stay, final night), then use portable scalp-EEG spot checks on other flagged events to confirm electrode positioning and gross morphology without full montage labor; escalate to full PSG only if the spot check reveals unexpected polyphasic or subclinical seizure-like features.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that semi-automated event detection promises to reduce manual review, but the escalation strategy determines how much clinical data justifies the tool's development. The options assume technician judgment or algorithmic thresholds are stable across a heterogeneous participant population, but none address that some parasomnia phenotypes (e.g., confusional arousals with brief vocalizations) may be inherently ambiguous in raw video and require full PSG context to adjudicate, making selective escalation risky for phenotypic validity.

levers.name: Participant stay duration and event-yield risk tolerance
levers.consequences: Shorter stays (2–4 weeks) minimize per-person cost and operational disruption but risk high unproductive admission rates if parasomnia events cluster unpredictably. Longer permissible stays (extending toward the 10-week ceiling) increase per-person cost and bed occupancy duration but improve probability of capturing at least one adjudicated event per participant, strengthening Aim 2 longitudinal characterization.
options: Adopt a tiered stay protocol: standard initial stay of 4 weeks for all participants, automatic extension to 6 weeks if fewer than one event is captured by day 25, then extension to 8 weeks if still zero events by day 40, capping at 10 weeks; this minimizes early-stay cost while protecting against unproductive admissions without requiring upfront commitment prediction.

Stratify baseline stay duration by pre-admission self-reported episode frequency and household corroboration: high-frequency self-reporters (≥4 episodes per month) receive 2-week baseline stay with extension to 4 weeks only if unproductive, while low-frequency reporters (2–3 episodes per month) begin at 4 weeks; this allocates stay duration based on prior signal and preserves bed availability for higher-yield candidates.

Offer participants a fixed 6-week stay with financial incentive structure (e.g., €50 base per night plus €100 event-capture bonus if ≥1 adjudicated event occurs) rather than extending by protocol; this sets clear participant expectations, concentrates recruitment messaging on 6-week commitment, and avoids the administrative complexity of iterative extension decisions.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that stay duration must balance yield against cost, but the pre-admission clinical assessment is coarse—a patient reporting '2 episodes per month' may be experiencing weekly clusters separated by weeks of silence, making stratification unreliable. The options assume extended stays proportionally increase capture probability, but none address the confound that longer stays may inadvertently select for psychological factors (e.g., higher tolerance for institutional living) that correlate with event frequency, potentially biasing the longitudinal characterization cohort.

levers.name: Matched control enrollment scale and benchmarking robustness
levers.consequences: A large control cohort (e.g., 30–50 healthy participants) provides robust baseline normative data for sensor false-positive rates and arousal patterns, enabling rigorous benchmarking of event-triage sensitivity and specificity. A minimal control cohort (5–10 participants) reduces recruitment and monitoring cost but yields sparse normative statistics, potentially undermining claims that semi-automated tools discriminate parasomnia from normal behavior.
options: Recruit controls in cohort waves synchronized to case admissions, targeting 1 control for every 1.5–2 cases; this spreads control costs across the 3-year timeline and allows early benchmarking iterations during algorithm development, but requires sustained separate recruitment pathway and scheduling coordination.

Oversample controls in year one (12–15 controls during the 15–20 participant pilot phase) to generate robust normative baselines quickly, then minimize year-two and year-three control recruitment; this front-loads cost and analysis burden but allows algorithm tuning with stable reference data and reduces later recruitment complexity.

Reciprocal task controls: enroll case participants on matched control nights during their pre-event screening before formal admission, collecting one night of baseline monitoring under identical conditions; this collapses recruitment overhead and ensures perfect physiological matching (same person, same facility) but limits cross-person normative statistics and requires participants to tolerate an extra uncompensated night.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that a robust control group is scientifically necessary for Aim 3 (benchmarking event-triage tools), but controls contribute no direct parasomnia data, creating funding opportunity cost. The options assume control data collection follows the same residential model, but none address that healthy controls may be systematically biased (e.g., self-selected for good sleep hygiene, younger, less psychiatric burden) compared to parasomnia patients, making normative false-positive rates estimated from controls potentially unrepresentative of the real clinical population's sensor noise profile.

levers.name: Dual-rater annotation division and workflow dependency
levers.consequences: Full dual-blind independent annotation of all events by two trained raters ensures inter-rater reliability reporting and robust adjudication but doubles scoring labor cost and creates scheduling bottlenecks if raters disagree, requiring third-party arbitration. Selective dual-rating (e.g., 30% of events independently scored, remainder by primary rater alone) reduces cost and accelerates analysis but risks underreporting true disagreement rates and may bias published inter-rater figures.
options: Implement asymmetric annotation: the night technician performs real-time preliminary scoring for operational event response and triage decisions, while two independent raters score the same events later in blinded fashion without access to technician annotations; disagreements trigger video-plus-EEG review consensus meeting led by the PI, and inter-rater agreement is reported on the subset where both raters had initial assignments.

Stratify annotation burden by event confidence: high-confidence events (clear motor activity, polyphasic EMG on infrared video, >80% technician certainty) are scored by one trained rater plus spot-check verification by the clinical psychologist; lower-confidence or ambiguous events undergo full dual-blind scoring, concentrating inter-rater reliability reporting on clinically uncertain cases only.

Establish a rolling quality-control schedule where every 10th event is dual-scored by both primary and backup rater to generate inter-rater agreement estimates, but all other events are primary-rater-only to manage cost; if month-to-month inter-rater kappa on the 10% sample falls below 0.75, escalate to full dual-rating until agreement recovers.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that dual-rating is methodologically necessary to defend Aim 2 phenotyping claims against publication scrutiny, but the full dual-blind workflow is labor-intensive and creates dependency delays when raters disagree. The options assume either technician preliminary scoring can be decoupled from final adjudication, or that selective dual-rating preserves enough agreement visibility; neither addresses that parasomnia events, especially confusional arousals, may be inherently ambiguous to trained scorers (e.g., does a brief vocalization count as a distinct event or continuation of prior arousal?), meaning inter-rater disagreement rates could be high even with full dual-rating, forcing a methodological acknowledgment rather than a workflow fix.

levers.name: Sensor-escalation training data and event-triage algorithm scope
levers.consequences: Training the semi-automated event-triage tool on the facility's own longitudinal dataset (case and control recordings) ensures algorithm relevance to local sensor suite and participant population but requires months of dual-human-scored ground truth to accumulate before model development, delaying Aim 3 progress. Training on external parasomnia databases or using transfer learning from published polysomnography datasets accelerates algorithm development but risks poor generalization if external data capture parameters differ substantially.
options: Build the triage model iteratively as cases accumulate: begin with manually annotated case data from the first 20–25 participants (approximately month 6–9), then train a logistic-regression or random-forest classifier to flag probable parasomnia episodes based on EEG+EMG+video features; freeze the model at month 12 and benchmark against subsequent cases, publishing sensitivity and specificity against dual-human agreement as the primary validation metric.

Initiate triage-model development using multi-center data from published sleep-EEG repositories (e.g., PHYSIONET, Epilepsy Ecosystem) to generate a baseline classifier trained on seizure-mimics and normal sleep, then fine-tune transfer-learning weights on the first 15–20 facility cases once ground truth is available; this allows algorithm testing by month 5–6 and reduces dependency on local data accumulation.

Constrain the initial triage model to a narrow feature set (e.g., EMG spiking + video motion intensity + arousal frequency band) chosen clinically by the team, then validate sensitivity and specificity on local data; defer multimodal deep-learning approaches until year two if the simpler model achieves >85% sensitivity, avoiding premature complexity while preserving option to elaborate later.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that algorithm development is a Aim 3 deliverable with 36-month deadline, but ground-truth training data only accumulates as cases enroll and dual-raters complete scoring, creating a temporal crunch. The options assume either local data suffices by month 12, external data transfers appropriately, or simple models suffice initially; none address that parasomnia events vary dramatically in morphology (sleepwalking with complex motor sequences vs. brief confusional arousal vocalization), potentially requiring separate sub-models for each phenotype, which would fragment the already-limited training set and may force a choice between a generalist model with modest performance or phenotype-specific models with insufficient data per type.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical partnership timing and scope versus publication autonomy optics
levers.consequences: Early partnership engagement (during year-one setup) provides potential co-funding for equipment or participant compensation, reducing DFG budget pressure, but signals to the sleep medicine community that industry influence may shape cohort selection or publication strategy. Late-stage partnership (year two, post-pilot results) preserves first-author academic autonomy in methods and Aim 2 publications but forgoes co-funding and requires the DFG/university funding to sustain 3 years independently without compromise.
options: Defer pharmaceutical partnership discussions until month 18–20, after pilot cohort completion and successful operational validation; present initial methods and feasibility results at a major conference or preprint, then approach pharma partners from a position of demonstrated capability and negotiating leverage, retaining full publication control over Aims 1–2 while licensing Aim 3 algorithm insights if desired.

Engage pharmaceutical partnership at project inception as optional co-funder for escalation-night PSG equipment or matching participant compensation, but contractually restrict partner rights to Aim 3 (algorithm benchmarking) only, explicitly excluding Aim 1 and Aim 2 data; publish all phenotyping and longitudinal results under lead authorship without pre-publication delay, offering pharma only a 30-day technical-accuracy review window.

Establish a steering committee that includes a pharma liaison as non-voting observer for monthly operational meetings, providing early transparency and relationship-building, but exclude pharma input from scientific decision gates (cohort definition, event adjudication, publication selection); frame this arrangement as 'partnership visibility' rather than partnership leverage, to maintain credibility with the academic sleep medicine establishment.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is that pharmaceutical funding could ease budget constraints and provide trial-readiness insights for future intervention studies, but accepting pharma capital too early invites skepticism about independence, especially in parasomnia research where pharma interest in novel pharmacotherapies is active. The options assume pharmaceutical interest is sustainable and non-coercive, but none address the scenario where pharma partners expect preferential data access for biomarker discovery (e.g., EEG microstructure linked to drug response) that falls outside Aims 1–3, forcing a renegotiation midway through the project or a publication embargo that contradicts the stated 30-day window.

levers.name: Residential atmosphere versus clinical safety infrastructure trade-off
levers.consequences: The facility must balance a domestic feel that elicits naturalistic parasomnia behavior against the safety systems (padded walls, door alarms, restricted windows) that suppress it or create artificiality. Installing and maintaining safety hardware may inadvertently cue participants to abnormal expectation, altering sleep architecture and parasomnia expression; conversely, minimizing visible safety measures increases injury risk if an event escalates. This tension directly affects data validity—if the environment feels too institutional, capture rates drop and Aim 2 phenotyping becomes underpowered.
options: Employ concealed safety engineering (soft-edge handrails disguised as décor elements, motion-responsive floor lighting positioned as ambient features, door locking on the exterior only) that protects without telegraphing surveillance or constraint, accepted after ethics review and explicit participant consent on setup.

Install visible but normalized safety fixtures (foam corner guards in styles and colors matching interior design, transparent acrylic window films, openly disclosed monitoring) as part of the intake briefing so participants mentally integrate them as standard sleep-lab convention rather than aberration.

Stratify suites into two operational tiers—four suites with maximal concealment for primary phenotyping cohort, and four with standard clinical safety—then compare event yield and sleep-stage metrics between tiers to empirically measure the atmospheric penalty and guide year-two design decisions.
levers.review: Minimizing visible safety measures trades short-term realism against long-term liability: if a participant suffers a preventable injury because safety systems were too subtle, ethics approval and institutional trust collapse. None of the options address the deeper problem—staff perception of 'acceptable risk' in a residential facility is more conservative than in a traditional hospital sleep lab, so even well-designed concealment may not overcome technician reluctance to reduce supervision during high-risk events.

levers.name: Event scarcity and capture efficiency versus cost-per-productive-night
levers.consequences: The inclusion criterion (≥2 episodes per month) implies an event density of roughly 0.07 episodes per night population-wide, yet many participants may have zero captured episodes during their stay, necessitating extension or replacement. Each unproductive admission consumes fixed overhead (suite utilities, staffing, technician annotation time) without yielding scientifically usable data; the protocol permits up to 20% unproductive stays before mandatory criteria review. This lever directly determines bed utilization, cost per captured event, and whether year-two scaling to 30–35 participants is economically sustainable.
options: Implement a two-phase admission strategy: first 7–10 nights under routine monitoring to measure actual event frequency in the residential setting, then gate stay extension on a documented episode or confirmed high-risk movement signature from mattress sensors, capping extensions only for participants showing clear signal.

Establish a pre-admission remote actigraphy and sleep diary screening period (4–6 weeks prior) to quantify home baseline episode frequency and refine inclusion thresholds, rejecting candidates whose actual rate falls below 1 episode per month and reserving extension beds only for participants whose home data predicted higher yield.

Adopt a rolling-cohort model with a standing 'event-capture reserve pool' of 5–10 pre-screened participants on 2–3 week call-up intervals, deployed only when active suites flag high-yield signals (e.g., mattress-sensor detected movement clusters matching parasomnia signature), maximizing event density per admission.
levers.review: Tightening pre-admission screening to reduce unproductive stays risks excluding participants with genuinely variable parasomnia (intermittent responders whose month-to-month frequency is unpredictable), potentially biasing the Aim 2 phenotyping cohort toward high-frequency easy cases. The call-up reserve pool assumes reliable short-notice participant availability, which recruitment in a regional population may not support; missed call-ups generate no revenue and strain participant goodwill.

levers.name: Data richness and physiological resolution versus privacy constraint and public deposit feasibility
levers.consequences: The tiered sensing model (nightly low-burden plus scheduled enhanced PSG plus escalation nights) maximizes event resolution but generates multimodal data including bedroom video, which carries legal and ethical restrictions that bar public repository deposit. Retaining bedroom video only for on-site annotation limits reproducibility and external validation of event-triage algorithms, since future researchers cannot audit the video-labeled ground truth; removing video entirely preserves privacy but sacrifices the gold-standard annotation resource for rare, morphologically complex events. This tension directly undermines Aim 3 benchmarking credibility.
options: Restrict video to the first night only (establishing baseline movement patterns and technician familiarity) and rely entirely on dual-rater PSG annotation plus mattress-sensor correlation for all subsequent events, depositing de-identified EEG, actigraphy, and computed event-flags in a public repository and offering conditional video access under a formal data use agreement reviewed case-by-case.

Implement a federated two-tier sharing model: deposit all low-burden sensor streams (EEG headband, mattress pressure, infrared body-position maps without face resolution, ambient sound spectrograms) openly; reserve full PSG and video in a secure institutional repository accessible to external collaborators upon ethics approval and institutional affiliation verification, documented in the public dataset as 'extended protocol available'.

Omit bedroom video entirely and substitute a second independent technician live-coding parasomnia morphology in real time (body position, limb movement, vocalization intensity, coherence with EEG arousal) at the bedside for the final-night and escalation sessions, storing only the coded behavioral descriptors rather than raw video, shareable under standard data use agreements.
levers.review: Removing video sacrifices nuance for events with ambiguous polysomnographic signatures (shallow arousals with prominent motor behavior that may be confusional arousal versus sleep-related movement disorder boundary case)—human scorers often resolve these by replaying video frame-by-frame. The federated model extends legal review timelines for external access and may deter replication studies; it also assumes the institutional repository remains actively stewarded for 10+ years, a commitment often abandoned post-funding.

levers.name: Autonomous event detection capability versus maintaining human scorer primacy
levers.consequences: Aim 3 explicitly benchmarks semi-automated event-triage tools against dual human scoring, not against 'ground truth' diagnosis, because parasomnia phenotyping is not yet standardized enough to claim algorithmic autonomy. However, if triage tools achieve high sensitivity and specificity (e.g., 85% agreement with human scorers), pressure will mount from funders, clinicians, and future industry partners to position them as diagnostically autonomous. Caving to this pressure risks publishing inflated performance claims on data that reflects human annotation variance rather than true accuracy, damaging credibility when the tool is prospectively tested on new populations.
options: Explicitly frame the triage model as a 'technician-assistance tool' in all publications and grant proposals, reporting only sensitivity and specificity relative to majority human-rater agreement and refusing to claim autonomous detection; design validation workflow to require one human reviewer to approve triage flags before clinical reporting, regardless of algorithm confidence.

Prospectively reserve a held-out test set (20% of captured events, withheld from model training) and at month 30 conduct a parallel annotation by a new independent human rater blind to both the algorithm output and the original dual-rater consensus, using four-way agreement patterns (algorithm, original pair, held-out rater) to transparently quantify where the model diverges from human consensus.

Publish two parallel results: one emphasizing triage sensitivity for identifying potential events (enabling technician review prioritization) and a separate report on specificity for ruling out false positives, clearly labeling each metric's clinical utility and limitations rather than combining them into a single 'accuracy' statistic that obscures conditional performance.
levers.review: Insisting on human primacy may under-utilize the computational model and frustrate collaborators expecting digital automation; conversely, overselling the model's maturity now creates pressure to deploy it clinically before adequate prospective validation, and sets up replication failure in external populations where the human-annotation style or event prevalence differs. The held-out rater strategy requires securing a third expert scorer for month 30, contingent on continued funding and their availability.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical partnership structure and academic independence
levers.consequences: A pharma partner can accelerate funding and provide domain expertise, but the default negotiated arrangement (30-day pre-publication review window only) is silent on downstream IP—what if a pharma-sponsored secondary analysis identifies a novel biomarker or therapeutic target? Ambiguity here may lead to post-hoc disputes over publication rights, institutional review, or derivative patent claims. The current framing correctly positions pharma as 'optional upside,' but failing to lock in governance terms upfront risks the partnership becoming a lever for funders or the sponsor to influence publication strategy under the guise of 'collaborative science.'
options: Establish a written Partnership Charter at engagement, specifying that all primary Aims 1–3 data, publications, and IP remain wholly the University's unless pharma co-authors manuscripts (triggering standard co-authorship IP sharing); pharma receives 30-day review, but University retains unilateral publication decision and all rights to methodological patents on the triage tool.

Decline any pharma partnership during the 3-year pilot and secure full funding via DFG + University internal mechanisms, then solicit industry co-investment only for a separate Phase Two protocol (year 4 onwards) targeting specific biomarkers or patient stratification, where pharma involvement is scoped upfront as a distinct study.

Offer pharma a tiered engagement: Year 1 sponsorship of the event-triage algorithm development (Aim 3) as a discrete task with co-authorship opportunity but no veto on primary-aim publications; Year 2 optional participation in a sub-study using residual samples for novel biomarker discovery, with pharma bearing full cost and University retaining IP on any diagnostically-actionable findings.
levers.review: Rejecting pharma entirely in a 3-year, €3.8M budget assumes DFG success in the 2026 cycle (currently uncertain) and forgoes potential acceleration; accepting pharma without a Charter risks subtle mission drift, where Year 2 planning unconsciously prioritizes phenotypes valuable to the sponsor over those most scientifically novel. The tiered model divides effort across two protocols, creating annotation and ethics-review parallelism overhead.

levers.name: Annotation throughput and inter-rater agreement sustainability
levers.consequences: The protocol mandates dual independent scoring of all PSG nights using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported; for 50–70 participants with an average of 3 enhanced PSG nights each (first, mid-stay, final), plus escalation nights for captured events, the scoring burden could exceed 400–500 nights of full PSG annotation. At €40–60 per night for expert scorers (clinical neurophysiologists), annual scoring costs alone are €16K–30K year one, rising to €25K–50K year two. If inter-rater agreement falls below 85% (Cohen's kappa), the protocol requires adjudication by a third party, further inflating cost. This lever directly determines whether Aim 2 phenotyping analysis is feasible and timely.
options: Implement a stepped annotation protocol: year-one nights scored by the two core postdocs (building internal agreement through biweekly consensus meetings), and only nights with disagreement >5% (kappa <0.80) are sent to external expert scorers for adjudication, then conduct pooled inter-rater reliability at year-end to justify or adjust the threshold.

Develop a hybrid annotation workflow where the night technician performs real-time AASM-aligned flagging during the recording (marking probable arousals, movement onsets, microarousals), then two independent offline scorers review the technician-flagged windows only (not the entire night), with technician confidence attached to each flag to weight scorer priority; kappa is computed on the flagged-window subset.

Outsource all scoring to a contracted sleep-medicine clinic partner (e.g., a regional hospital with existing PSG-scoring infrastructure) under a per-night fee-for-service agreement and a shared protocol checklist, trading some quality-control overhead for predictable monthly costs and separation of operational and scientific roles, with monthly spot-check agreement audits.
levers.review: Relying on postdocs for primary scoring works in year one but jeopardizes continuity if either postdoc leaves; the biweekly consensus process slows annotation velocity and may inadvertently create a third 'reference' scorer that inflates apparent agreement. The hybrid technician-flagging model reduces offline scoring time but risks embedding technician bias into the flagging layer, systematically improving agreement in unhelpful ways (technician and scorers converging on the same mistake). Outsourcing saves salary costs but surrenders day-to-day quality oversight and assumes the partner clinic has consistent AASM expertise—regional variation in sleep-stage scoring is real.

levers.name: Phased suite expansion and demand forecasting versus upfront capital commitment
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites pending year-one data on event-capture rates, staffing safety, and annotation throughput; however, if recruitment demand exceeds 8-suite capacity and a waiting list forms, the facility sacrifices scientifically important participants and funding opportunity in year two. Conversely, expanding to 12 suites prematurely locks in additional lease and staffing costs (€80K–120K annually) if participant flow remains modest. This lever determines facility utilization efficiency and year-two budget realism.
options: Commit to full 12-suite fit-out in year-one renovation (requiring €150K–200K additional capital for cabling, monitor infrastructure, safety systems) but operationalize only 8 suites initially; pre-wire the four dark suites and conduct monthly utilization forecasting (queued referrals, event-capture yield, staff utilization) with a formal decision gate at month 12 to activate suites 9–12 if capacity utilization exceeds 75% and event-capture rate meets Aim 2 thresholds.

Build out only the 8-suite core in year-one and execute a streamlined decision protocol at month 18: if enrolled participants >30, queued referrals >10, and captured events >50, trigger a 6-month expansion contract to add suites 9–12 by month 24, accepting a 6-month waiting period for incremental participants rather than forecasting demand upfront.

Negotiate with the residential property landlord a contingent lease amendment allowing month-by-month suite occupancy scaling (e.g., rent billed per active suite used rather than per total available), eliminating capital commitment to unused infrastructure and allowing suite additions on 60-day notice without construction, traded against higher per-suite lease cost (5–10% premium).
levers.review: Pre-wiring all 12 suites avoids construction disruption mid-study but commits €150K+ even if demand materializes slowly, potentially exhausting year-one contingency funds. The rolling decision-gate approach respects uncertainty but may find that activation at month 18 comes too late to meaningfully expand year-two cohort size (ethics amendments, participant recruitment pipeline, staff hiring all require 2–3 months lead time). The per-suite variable lease preserves flexibility but higher marginal rent may erode margins and give landlord perverse incentive to deny expansion notice.

levers.name: Capturing rare events without extended institutional stays
levers.consequences: Longer residential stays (2–8 weeks) maximize event yield but create substantial participant burden, restrict recruitment to highly motivated individuals, and occupy bed capacity longer per enrolled subject. Conversely, shorter standardized stays reduce operational friction but risk unproductive admissions — if the target 70–80% capture rate drops below 50%, the facility consumes resources without scientific return, and the month-18 failure trigger (fewer than 40 adjudicated events) becomes harder to meet.
options: Mandate a tiered stay protocol: all participants begin with a baseline 2-week stay; only those with zero captured events extend to week 3–8, with extension approval conditional on technician-flagged candidate events occurring by the end of week 2 (preserving bed turnover while retargeting high-variance individuals).

Recruit a parallel remote-monitoring cohort using home-deployed dry EEG and mattress sensors without facility residence, accepting degraded physiological resolution in exchange for larger denominator and longer natural-history observation windows to feed the event-triage algorithm even if formal event adjudication remains limited to in-facility stays.

Implement weekly in-facility 'capture sessions' lasting 3–4 consecutive nights with participants returning to daily life between sessions over 6–8 weeks total, attempting to harvest the behavioral ecology of home while maintaining facility control and compressing total bed occupancy per participant.
levers.review: Rare-event capture is fundamentally probabilistic and participant-dependent; the plan assumes 70–80% yield based on a self-reported 2-episodes-per-month screening criterion, but this criterion may overestimate true frequency in a new unfamiliar environment (novel setting often suppresses parasomnia expression). None of the options directly addresses whether the pre-admission assessment pipeline can accurately predict which screened individuals will actually produce events, risking systematic over-recruitment of low-yield participants and burndown of the €80-per-night compensation budget.

levers.name: Technician safety workload and event-response capability
levers.consequences: Covering 8 active suites with three rotating night technicians (one per shift, two off) provides adequate margin for annotation and event intervention, but scales poorly if suites expand or event frequency is higher than projected. Understaffing creates annotation backlog and delayed event escalation; overstaffing during low-event periods wastes personnel budget. The plan does not specify a target response time from technician alert to bedside, which affects clinical safety and event data completeness.
options: Lock the technician-to-suite ratio at 1:4 (one technician per 4 active suites) and conduct quarterly safety audits; if actual event rates exceed one event per technician per night on average, hire an additional part-time overnight shift supervisor rather than expanding suites, accepting that suite expansion is deferred indefinitely until staffing capacity is demonstrably surplus.

Deploy a hybrid model where a trained sleep technician covers 6–8 suites for clinical safety and immediate escalation, with a separate part-time data annotator (0.5 FTE, daytime) handling real-time scoring review and flagging ambiguous cases for dual-scorer triage, shifting annotation burden away from the critical-path overnight shift.

Establish a pre-agreed call-list of on-site emergency physician or neurologist coverage for nights when event frequency exceeds one per technician, formalized in an MOU with the affiliated hospital's neurology department, ensuring clinical escalation capacity without permanent headcount addition.
levers.review: The plan specifies three research technicians but does not define event-response protocols or acceptable response latency; 'safe staffing ratios' is cited as a month-18 success criterion but no numerical target is stated. None of the options explicitly prices in the cognitive load of real-time AASM-criteria scoring during a live event (distinguishing sleep terror from confusional arousal while the participant is confused is cognitively demanding), and all three assume the technician can adequately monitor video feeds and sensor streams simultaneously, which may degrade under fatigue during weeks of low-event nights.

levers.name: Dual-rater independence and annotation throughput sustainability
levers.consequences: Requiring two independent raters to score all captured events ensures inter-rater reliability and AASM-criteria rigor, but at a high labor cost per hour: typical dual-rater video-EEG scoring requires 4–8 hours per hour of recorded content depending on signal complexity. With 8 suites running continuously, even at a 40% capture rate, dual scoring of all nights (versus selective nights) can quickly exhaust budget and delay algorithmic development (Aim 3) which depends on complete labeled datasets.
options: Score only first-night, mid-stay, and final-night PSG montages in full dual-rater mode, treating those as the 'gold standard' events; technician-flagged candidate events on low-burden nights receive single-rater screening with dual-rater adjudication triggered only for algorithm-evaluation cohorts, accepting that longitudinal event frequency estimates will rely on technician flagging rather than formal dual scoring.

Contract an external sleep medicine center to provide the second independent rater, shifting annotation cost into a fixed subcontract and decoupling the facility's operational team from the scoring bottleneck; publish inter-rater agreement quarterly and declare analysis frozen if agreement falls below a pre-specified threshold (e.g., 80% concordance on event presence/absence).

Implement a three-tier annotation hierarchy: tier-one rapid technician triage (event present/absent/ambiguous), tier-two single-rater formal scoring for all flagged events, tier-three dual-rater adjudication only for a random 30% of scored events plus all disagreements, using agreement from the sampled subset to extrapolate inter-rater reliability across the full dataset.
levers.review: Dual-rater scoring is scientifically necessary for validity but the plan does not specify how many events are expected to be captured overall or whether the budget (€2M personnel over 3 years) allocates sufficient independent-rater time for full coverage. None of the options accounts for rater fatigue or drift over months of repetitive scoring, both of which will degrade agreement; the plan also assumes that the 'two independent raters' are readily available, but in a small sleep medicine field with limited expertise in parasomnia video analysis, finding truly independent, available raters may be operationally difficult within the Bonn region.

levers.name: Semi-automated event-triage algorithm training scope and cross-validation strategy
levers.consequences: Developing a benchmarked semi-automated triage tool (Aim 3) requires a labeled training set of captured-event cases; larger training sets improve algorithm generalization, but also demand extended data collection and dual-rater labeling time. Training only on facility events risks overfitting to facility-specific sensor noise and participant behaviors, whereas incorporating the remote-monitoring cohort (if adopted) provides diversity but introduces label ambiguity since remote events cannot be formally adjudicated in real time.
options: Restrict algorithm training to dual-rated in-facility events only, capping training set size at the number of events captured during the 3-year pilot; publish algorithm performance as facility-specific (sensitivity/specificity against dual-rater agreement on the captured cohort) and position the method as a 'prototype semi-automated event-flagging pipeline,' deferring multi-site external validation to a future phase-two grant.

Prospectively enroll 20–25 event-free control participants in the facility under identical protocols, generating a large labeled dataset of false-positive sensor activity and non-parasomnia arousals; train the algorithm on a balanced dataset of parasomnia events versus negative controls, enabling threshold optimization that explicitly minimizes false positives and improving real-world applicability.
