Collaborate with a European sleep medicine consortium (e.g., Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin member centers) to share anonymized event clips and dual-rater scores, pooling training data across multiple sites in exchange for co-authorship and algorithm access; accept reduced publication timeline (algorithm development extends to month 30) but gain multi-site generalization.
levers.review: The plan states the algorithm will be 'evaluated against dual human scorer agreement,' but does not specify whether the facility will reserve a held-out test set or if all captured events will be used for both training and evaluation, risking inflated performance estimates. Outcome class imbalance (many non-events, few parasomnia events) will skew standard metrics, and none of the options explicitly addresses how to handle ambiguous events (technician-flagged but not confirmed at dual review), which may represent the highest-value targets for algorithm assistance yet carry no ground truth.

levers.name: Privacy constraints and public data repository deposit feasibility
levers.consequences: Bedroom video is explicitly excluded from public repositories due to privacy risk, but this limits the richness of openly-shared data and reduces scientific transparency and reproducibility. Physiological-only deposits (EEG, sensors, arousal annotations) are feasible but provide no context for validating event classification, potentially limiting downstream reuse by other groups. The plan does not specify which physiological data will be deposited (full PSG, headband-only, or sensor subset) or timelines for de-identification and repository submission.
options: Deposit only de-identified low-burden nightly headband EEG, mattress sensor, and acoustic event annotations (without raw video) in a public BIDS repository, enabling algorithm reuse and longitudinal pattern analysis while preserving participant privacy; acknowledge in publications that behavioral context is unavailable and external researchers cannot independently validate event morphology from video.

Create a tiered-access data enclave: deposit anonymized physiological data and event summaries in a public repository, but house raw video and detailed event clips in a managed access portal (e.g., European Genome-Phenome Archive) requiring data-use agreements and ethics approval per request, reducing privacy risk while preserving scientific scrutiny.

Obtain explicit broad consent during enrollment for video deposit under a participant-controlled anonymization option: offer participants the choice to permit video sharing with face blurring and identifier removal, and deposit only from opt-in participants; expect 30–40% opt-in rate and deposit a smaller but fully transparent video-enriched dataset, framing partial transparency as superior to full opacity.
levers.review: The plan identifies video privacy as a 'severe constraint' and bans public video deposit, but does not specify how bedroom context will be documented for future replication or how external researchers will validate event-classification disagreements; this trades transparency for privacy protection. None of the options explores whether the institutional review board or ethics committee would permit conditional video sharing under strong anonymization (face-blurred, temporally cropped), and the third option assumes 30–40% opt-in consent is achievable without evidence from similar parasomnia cohorts, which tend toward lower video-sharing acceptance due to the intimate nature of the setting.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical industry partnership timing and scope versus academic independence messaging
levers.consequences: An optional pharma partnership can provide upside funding and potential drug-testing collaboration, but introduces contractual obligations (e.g., the 30-day pre-publication review window) that may delay publication and create perception of industry bias even if academically protected. Entering into partnership early (year 1) secures funding but locks in requirements; delaying partnership until year 2 or 3 risks losing interested sponsors but preserves autonomy longer and allows methodological validation first.
options: Decline pharma partnership at launch and pursue DFG plus University of Bonn internal funding exclusively; preserve unrestricted publication timeline and frame the facility as purely academic-mission-driven in recruitment materials, maximizing participant trust; revisit pharma collaboration only after methods-validation publications (Aim 1) establish credibility, positioning the facility as a de-risk scientific partner rather than a captive contract-research site.

Solicit a pharma partnership letter of intent by month 6, specifying that funding comes in year 2 only and contingent on clearance by the scientific advisory board; draft a partnership agreement that guarantees publication rights after 60 days regardless of pharma comment, and require pharma to co-fund only non-core operational costs (e.g., algorithm development, control-group expansion) rather than core safety and enrollment infrastructure.

Establish a separate RBD-specific research track formally co-sponsored by a neurology-focused pharmaceutical partner (neurodegenerative disease interest), isolating industry involvement to the secondary RBD protocol and keeping the primary NREM parasomnia track entirely under DFG/university funding; this compartmentalization preserves academic independence claims for the main cohort while accepting industry involvement in an exploratory arm.
levers.review: The plan positions pharma partnership as 'optional upside' and claims the academic team 'retains full publication rights,' but does not specify whether the 30-day comment window creates a de facto publication veto (if pharma objects, will the team proceed despite objection?), nor does it address how participants and recruitment networks will perceive industry involvement if announced during enrollment. The third option assumes that compartmentalizing the RBD arm will prevent spillover perception of bias, but in the field, reputation attaches to the entire research unit regardless of funding source segmentation.

levers.name: Facility expansion decision gates and participant demand forecasting
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites pending pilot success, but does not specify which success criteria unlock expansion (month 18 triggers review of enrollment <25 or event capture <40, but neither directly addresses suite utilization). If demand exceeds 8-suite capacity before month 18, the facility cannot scale and risks losing recruited participants; if demand is weak, expansion wastes capital on infrastructure that remains idle. Recruitment projections (15–20 participants year 1, 30–35 year 2) assume constant intake rate without explaining seasonal variation or recruitment ramp-up.
options: Adopt a rolling-enrollment expansion trigger: commit to 12-suite renovation by month 15 if enrollment reaches 20 participants by month 12 and technician safety audits confirm no safety deficits at 8-suite staffing; stagger the renovation across months 15–18 to minimize operational disruption and accept the risk that expansion capital may be committed before outcome-quality metrics (inter-rater agreement, algorithm performance) are final.

Implement a capacity-management protocol using a 6-month rolling wait-list: if prospective participants exceed 8-suite capacity, place them on a publicly-tracked waitlist with priority based on event frequency and clinical urgency; measure wait-list length monthly and trigger expansion discussion if >10 individuals are waiting; this defers expansion capital until demand is demonstrably sustained, but risks participant attrition if waits exceed 4 months.

Negotiate an option with the landlord or adjacent property owner to secure a third building (4 additional suites) with lease commencement at month 18, contingent on a go/no-go decision informed by month-18 external review data; this preserves expansion optionality without upfront capital commitment but reduces timing control and risks losing the property option if expansion criteria are not met by month 18.
levers.review: Recruitment projections (15–20 year 1, 30–35 year 2) show a 50–75% ramp, but do not account for seasonal variation in parasomnia event reporting, delay between referral and enrollment (specialist assessment, screening PSG, pre-admission workup can take 2–3 months), or the fact that the NREM parasomnia population in Germany is poorly characterized, making demand forecasting speculative. None of the options addresses whether the pre-admission exclusion criteria (no nocturnal epilepsy, no untreated sleep apnea, no severe psychiatric instability) will narrow the referred pool more than expected, and the month-18 external review is the only circuit-breaker for course correction, which may be too late to salvage expansion timelines if capacity constraints emerge earlier.

levers.name: Domestic ambiance preservation versus safety-driven renovation depth
levers.consequences: Extensive safety modifications (padded corridors, restricted windows, silent alarms, fire-rated materials) reduce risk and satisfy ethics committees but introduce clinical cues that may suppress naturalistic parasomnia expression. Conversely, minimal intervention preserves authentic sleep environment and behavior capture fidelity but increases liability exposure and may jeopardize recruitment if families perceive inadequate protection.
options: Implement staged renovation with core safety interventions (window restrictors, discrete corridor padding, inaudible alarm systems) phased in before participant arrival rather than completed during setup, allowing naturalistic baseline photography and family reassurance messaging to emphasize 'home-like with safety overlays' rather than 'clinical conversion'.

Employ removable or concealable safety infrastructure—magnetic corner guards, portable mattress rails, temporary threshold modifications—installed immediately before check-in so suites photograph and feel residential for marketing, then decorate with family-comfort elements (artwork, personal refrigerator, soft lighting) that dominate first visual impression.

Accept higher perceived clinical finish in the common areas (reception, monitoring station, dining) but invest disproportionately in suite interiors with residential furnishings, artwork, and acoustic treatments so participants spend private hours in non-institutional space, segregating the 'clinical' workflow from sleeping quarters.
levers.review: The core tension is that ethics committees demand observable safety while participant recruitment messaging relies on feeling 'not like a hospital.' The options all assume removable or perceptual tactics work, but none address the hard constraint that fire safety codes in a converted residential building may mandate irreversible structural changes (e.g., fire-rated door frames, wall treatments) that cannot be hidden; this could force a choice between passing inspection and preserving ambiance, not both.

levers.name: Escalation-night deployment frequency versus false-alarm cost containment
levers.consequences: Deploying full PSG immediately after every technician-flagged event captures clinical-grade detail but generates high sensor logistics overhead, technician fatigue from rapid equipment staging, and analyst cost for processing nocturnal false alarms (snoring, restless legs, normal arousals). Conversely, rationing escalation nights to only high-confidence flags reduces cost but risks missing secondary or subtle recurrences that refine phenotyping.
options: Implement a mandatory 24-hour review window before escalation-night decision: technician flags events nocturnally, then morning raw-data review by the clinical psychologist and PI determines escalation; this adds one morning interpretation step but filters low-confidence signals and concentrates PSG nights on episodes with higher post-hoc confirmation probability.

Establish participant-specific escalation thresholds during week one based on each person's typical false-alarm rate and event morphology, then automatically schedule full PSG after every N-th flagged event for that participant; this personalizes cost allocation and captures recurrence patterns without human gate-keeping each decision.

Restrict full PSG escalation nights to events captured on already-scheduled enhanced nights (first night, mid-stay, final night), then use portable scalp-EEG spot checks on other flagged events to confirm electrode positioning and gross morphology without full montage labor; escalate to full PSG only if the spot check reveals unexpected polyphasic or subclinical seizure-like features.
levers.review: The core tension is that semi-automated event detection promises to reduce manual review, but the escalation strategy determines how much clinical data justifies the tool's development. The options assume technician judgment or algorithmic thresholds are stable across a heterogeneous participant population, but none address that some parasomnia phenotypes (e.g., confusional arousals with brief vocalizations) may be inherently ambiguous in raw video and require full PSG context to adjudicate, making selective escalation risky for phenotypic validity.

levers.name: Participant stay duration and event-yield risk tolerance
levers.consequences: Shorter stays (2–4 weeks) minimize per-person cost and operational disruption but risk high unproductive admission rates if parasomnia events cluster unpredictably. Longer permissible stays (extending toward the 10-week ceiling) increase per-person cost and bed occupancy duration but improve probability of capturing at least one adjudicated event per participant, strengthening Aim 2 longitudinal characterization.
options: Adopt a tiered stay protocol: standard initial stay of 4 weeks for all participants, automatic extension to 6 weeks if fewer than one event is captured by day 25, then extension to 8 weeks if still zero events by day 40, capping at 10 weeks; this minimizes early-stay cost while protecting against unproductive admissions without requiring upfront commitment prediction.

Stratify baseline stay duration by pre-admission self-reported episode frequency and household corroboration: high-frequency self-reporters (≥4 episodes per month) receive 2-week baseline stay with extension to 4 weeks only if unproductive, while low-frequency reporters (2–3 episodes per month) begin at 4 weeks; this allocates stay duration based on prior signal and preserves bed availability for higher-yield candidates.

Offer participants a fixed 6-week stay with financial incentive structure (e.g., €50 base per night plus €100 event-capture bonus if ≥1 adjudicated event occurs) rather than extending by protocol; this sets clear participant expectations, concentrates recruitment messaging on 6-week commitment, and avoids the administrative complexity of iterative extension decisions.
levers.review: The core tension is that stay duration must balance yield against cost, but the pre-admission clinical assessment is coarse—a patient reporting '2 episodes per month' may be experiencing weekly clusters separated by weeks of silence, making stratification unreliable. The options assume extended stays proportionally increase capture probability, but none address the confound that longer stays may inadvertently select for psychological factors (e.g., higher tolerance for institutional living) that correlate with event frequency, potentially biasing the longitudinal characterization cohort.

levers.name: Matched control enrollment scale and benchmarking robustness
levers.consequences: A large control cohort (e.g., 30–50 healthy participants) provides robust baseline normative data for sensor false-positive rates and arousal patterns, enabling rigorous benchmarking of event-triage sensitivity and specificity. A minimal control cohort (5–10 participants) reduces recruitment and monitoring cost but yields sparse normative statistics, potentially undermining claims that semi-automated tools discriminate parasomnia from normal behavior.
options: Recruit controls in cohort waves synchronized to case admissions, targeting 1 control for every 1.5–2 cases; this spreads control costs across the 3-year timeline and allows early benchmarking iterations during algorithm development, but requires sustained separate recruitment pathway and scheduling coordination.

Oversample controls in year one (12–15 controls during the 15–20 participant pilot phase) to generate robust normative baselines quickly, then minimize year-two and year-three control recruitment; this front-loads cost and analysis burden but allows algorithm tuning with stable reference data and reduces later recruitment complexity.

Reciprocal task controls: enroll case participants on matched control nights during their pre-event screening before formal admission, collecting one night of baseline monitoring under identical conditions; this collapses recruitment overhead and ensures perfect physiological matching (same person, same facility) but limits cross-person normative statistics and requires participants to tolerate an extra uncompensated night.
levers.review: The core tension is that a robust control group is scientifically necessary for Aim 3 (benchmarking event-triage tools), but controls contribute no direct parasomnia data, creating funding opportunity cost. The options assume control data collection follows the same residential model, but none address that healthy controls may be systematically biased (e.g., self-selected for good sleep hygiene, younger, less psychiatric burden) compared to parasomnia patients, making normative false-positive rates estimated from controls potentially unrepresentative of the real clinical population's sensor noise profile.

levers.name: Dual-rater annotation division and workflow dependency
levers.consequences: Full dual-blind independent annotation of all events by two trained raters ensures inter-rater reliability reporting and robust adjudication but doubles scoring labor cost and creates scheduling bottlenecks if raters disagree, requiring third-party arbitration. Selective dual-rating (e.g., 30% of events independently scored, remainder by primary rater alone) reduces cost and accelerates analysis but risks underreporting true disagreement rates and may bias published inter-rater figures.
options: Implement asymmetric annotation: the night technician performs real-time preliminary scoring for operational event response and triage decisions, while two independent raters score the same events later in blinded fashion without access to technician annotations; disagreements trigger video-plus-EEG review consensus meeting led by the PI, and inter-rater agreement is reported on the subset where both raters had initial assignments.

Stratify annotation burden by event confidence: high-confidence events (clear motor activity, polyphasic EMG on infrared video, >80% technician certainty) are scored by one trained rater plus spot-check verification by the clinical psychologist; lower-confidence or ambiguous events undergo full dual-blind scoring, concentrating inter-rater reliability reporting on clinically uncertain cases only.

Establish a rolling quality-control schedule where every 10th event is dual-scored by both primary and backup rater to generate inter-rater agreement estimates, but all other events are primary-rater-only to manage cost; if month-to-month inter-rater kappa on the 10% sample falls below 0.75, escalate to full dual-rating until agreement recovers.
levers.review: The core tension is that dual-rating is methodologically necessary to defend Aim 2 phenotyping claims against publication scrutiny, but the full dual-blind workflow is labor-intensive and creates dependency delays when raters disagree. The options assume either technician preliminary scoring can be decoupled from final adjudication, or that selective dual-rating preserves enough agreement visibility; neither addresses that parasomnia events, especially confusional arousals, may be inherently ambiguous to trained scorers (e.g., does a brief vocalization count as a distinct event or continuation of prior arousal?), meaning inter-rater disagreement rates could be high even with full dual-rating, forcing a methodological acknowledgment rather than a workflow fix.

levers.name: Sensor-escalation training data and event-triage algorithm scope
levers.consequences: Training the semi-automated event-triage tool on the facility's own longitudinal dataset (case and control recordings) ensures algorithm relevance to local sensor suite and participant population but requires months of dual-human-scored ground truth to accumulate before model development, delaying Aim 3 progress. Training on external parasomnia databases or using transfer learning from published polysomnography datasets accelerates algorithm development but risks poor generalization if external data capture parameters differ substantially.
options: Build the triage model iteratively as cases accumulate: begin with manually annotated case data from the first 20–25 participants (approximately month 6–9), then train a logistic-regression or random-forest classifier to flag probable parasomnia episodes based on EEG+EMG+video features; freeze the model at month 12 and benchmark against subsequent cases, publishing sensitivity and specificity against dual-human agreement as the primary validation metric.

Initiate triage-model development using multi-center data from published sleep-EEG repositories (e.g., PHYSIONET, Epilepsy Ecosystem) to generate a baseline classifier trained on seizure-mimics and normal sleep, then fine-tune transfer-learning weights on the first 15–20 facility cases once ground truth is available; this allows algorithm testing by month 5–6 and reduces dependency on local data accumulation.

Constrain the initial triage model to a narrow feature set (e.g., EMG spiking + video motion intensity + arousal frequency band) chosen clinically by the team, then validate sensitivity and specificity on local data; defer multimodal deep-learning approaches until year two if the simpler model achieves >85% sensitivity, avoiding premature complexity while preserving option to elaborate later.
levers.review: The core tension is that algorithm development is a Aim 3 deliverable with 36-month deadline, but ground-truth training data only accumulates as cases enroll and dual-raters complete scoring, creating a temporal crunch. The options assume either local data suffices by month 12, external data transfers appropriately, or simple models suffice initially; none address that parasomnia events vary dramatically in morphology (sleepwalking with complex motor sequences vs. brief confusional arousal vocalization), potentially requiring separate sub-models for each phenotype, which would fragment the already-limited training set and may force a choice between a generalist model with modest performance or phenotype-specific models with insufficient data per type.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical partnership timing and scope versus publication autonomy optics
levers.consequences: Early partnership engagement (during year-one setup) provides potential co-funding for equipment or participant compensation, reducing DFG budget pressure, but signals to the sleep medicine community that industry influence may shape cohort selection or publication strategy. Late-stage partnership (year two, post-pilot results) preserves first-author academic autonomy in methods and Aim 2 publications but forgoes co-funding and requires the DFG/university funding to sustain 3 years independently without compromise.
options: Defer pharmaceutical partnership discussions until month 18–20, after pilot cohort completion and successful operational validation; present initial methods and feasibility results at a major conference or preprint, then approach pharma partners from a position of demonstrated capability and negotiating leverage, retaining full publication control over Aims 1–2 while licensing Aim 3 algorithm insights if desired.

Engage pharmaceutical partnership at project inception as optional co-funder for escalation-night PSG equipment or matching participant compensation, but contractually restrict partner rights to Aim 3 (algorithm benchmarking) only, explicitly excluding Aim 1 and Aim 2 data; publish all phenotyping and longitudinal results under lead authorship without pre-publication delay, offering pharma only a 30-day technical-accuracy review window.

Establish a steering committee that includes a pharma liaison as non-voting observer for monthly operational meetings, providing early transparency and relationship-building, but exclude pharma input from scientific decision gates (cohort definition, event adjudication, publication selection); frame this arrangement as 'partnership visibility' rather than partnership leverage, to maintain credibility with the academic sleep medicine establishment.
levers.review: The core tension is that pharmaceutical funding could ease budget constraints and provide trial-readiness insights for future intervention studies, but accepting pharma capital too early invites skepticism about independence, especially in parasomnia research where pharma interest in novel pharmacotherapies is active. The options assume pharmaceutical interest is sustainable and non-coercive, but none address the scenario where pharma partners expect preferential data access for biomarker discovery (e.g., EEG microstructure linked to drug response) that falls outside Aims 1–3, forcing a renegotiation midway through the project or a publication embargo that contradicts the stated 30-day window.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but the options neglect that a single hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
